Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd watch a ton of video lectures in one sitting, maybe for a few hours, and then I wouldn't touch the course for days. I'm a visual learner so I'd often pause videos and sketch out diagrams or create mental maps, but I rarely planned when I'd actually sit down to study. It was more like whenever I felt the energy or remembered I had coursework to do.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines rather than any real schedule. I'd notice an assignment was due in a couple days and suddenly I'd be cramming everything at once. Some weeks I'd do nothing, other weeks I'd be up late trying to catch up. I never really established a consistent weekly rhythm, even though I probably should have.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification that scared me into action, or honestly, boredom. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if anything new was posted or to check my grades. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was something almost addictive about seeing my progress and earning those little achievements. It made the platform feel less like a chore.
Q4: For a while, the gamified elements made checking the LMS kind of habitual. I'd log in to see my points or badges, which sounds silly but it worked on me. It became part of my procrastination routine in a weird way—I'd check the LMS before checking social media. But that habit faded when the novelty wore off and real life got busier.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to take notes or rewind confusing parts. The discussion forums were occasionally useful too when other students explained concepts in different ways. I also liked when there were visual diagrams or infographics embedded in the materials—those stuck with me way better than walls of text.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and any long PDF documents. My eyes would just glaze over trying to read dense text on a screen. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read them. The discussion posts I only engaged with minimally—I'd do the required posts but wouldn't really go back to follow conversations unless I was desperate for clarification.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it had some visual component that kept me engaged. The gamified tasks were great because there was instant feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt like busywork with no clear payoff, I'd put it off indefinitely. I needed to feel like I was actually learning something or making progress.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required ones had that deadline pressure that forced me to act, but optional ones just never made it to my priority list. Even if they seemed interesting, there was always something more urgent demanding my attention. The gamified optional tasks were different though—they felt more like a game than homework.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download the video lectures and watch them offline on my tablet, usually lying in bed or at a coffee shop. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp. My handwritten notes and diagrams were a huge part of my studying, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd sometimes study from photos I took of my notes on my phone.
Q10: There were many times I'd download materials and then spend hours studying them without being connected to the LMS at all. I'd rewatch downloaded videos, review my handwritten notes, or work through problems on paper. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over text, which was definitely learning, but the LMS had no idea. My actual study time was way more than what my login patterns would suggest.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video lecture once to download it, then watch it three or four more times offline over the next week. The LMS probably showed I viewed it once, but the reality was much different. Same with PDFs—I'd download them and if I actually read them, it was on my tablet disconnected from everything. One view in the system could mean hours of actual engagement.
Q12: Around midterms I hit a wall where everything felt overwhelming. I had multiple courses demanding attention, some personal stuff going on, and I just couldn't bring myself to engage with anything academic. My attendance dropped even more during that period. The stress was medium-level but constant, which was almost worse than acute stress because it just drained me slowly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had competing responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later kept getting pushed back. Confusion also played a role—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than face the frustration. Workload from other courses often meant this one got deprioritized even though I found the content interesting.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open while scrolling through my phone, or I'd be watching a lecture while my mind was completely elsewhere. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them as complete without actually absorbing anything. Those sessions felt hollow—I was technically present but mentally checked out. It was engagement theater, not real learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The assignment scores show that—I'd rush through them last minute and the quality suffered. I think it was partly anxiety about not doing them perfectly, so I'd avoid starting. Also, if something seemed boring or tedious, I'd find any excuse to do it later. My resilience for pushing through unpleasant tasks is pretty low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS—logging in frequently, watching videos at 1.5x speed, frantically completing tasks. My engagement was extremely spiky because of this. The gamified deadlines helped a bit because they felt less threatening somehow.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to engage more, while harsh or confusing feedback made me want to avoid the course entirely. When I got positive comments on discussion posts, I'd actually want to participate more. But if I got a low grade without clear explanation, I'd feel defeated and disengage. My mood after interventions was usually good, which kept me coming back for more.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I have terrible self-discipline. Having 14 online courses meant I was constantly juggling and this one sometimes fell through the cracks. The gamified elements added some structure that I actually responded to, giving me smaller goals to hit.
Q19: The visual elements and gamification made engagement way easier for me. Seeing progress bars fill up or earning badges gave me little dopamine hits that kept me coming back. What made it harder was the sheer volume of reading materials and the lack of interactive visual content in some modules. When everything was text-based, I'd zone out quickly.
Q20: I think that could be someone exploring the platform, maybe watching videos or reading materials, but struggling with actually producing work. Or they could be anxious about submitting imperfect work, so they engage with content but freeze when it comes to assignments. It could also be someone who's genuinely trying to understand the material but hitting a wall when applying it.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience pretty well. My exam score was high even though my attendance was terrible because I found ways to learn that didn't involve being visibly active in the LMS. I'd download materials, study offline, use external resources. The LMS saw low activity but I was still putting in work—just not in ways the system could track. Quality over quantity of logins.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether you're actually thinking while watching a video or just letting it play. They miss offline studying, discussions with friends, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They see the surface but not the substance.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being curious, asking questions in your head, connecting ideas. For me, engagement feels like when I'm drawing diagrams and suddenly something clicks. It's an internal state that doesn't always match external activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned from downloaded materials, or the conversations I had with classmates outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my genuine interest in certain topics that I explored through external resources. It also wouldn't show my emotional journey—the anxiety before deadlines, the satisfaction after understanding something, the guilt during my disengaged periods. The human experience gets flattened into clicks and timestamps.
